straight_beat.name: Russ Havelock
straight_beat.column:
The D-backs’ 5-4 win over the Phillies yesterday was a game that felt like a walk-off moment in itself. Not because of the final score, but because of the way it unfolded—a tight, back-and-forth contest that ended with the kind of drama that makes you want to rewind the tape. The team’s ability to claw back from deficits, again and again, has become a hallmark of this season. It’s not just about the wins; it’s about the resilience in the face of adversity. You could argue that the D-backs have mastered the art of the comeback, turning potential losses into momentum.  

Let’s talk about the late-inning heroics. The team’s bullpen, often criticized for inconsistency, stepped up when it mattered most. Closer Daniel Corcino, in particular, has been a steadying force in the ninth inning. His ability to navigate high-leverage situations—like the one yesterday where he faced a bases-loaded, two-out scenario—has become a point of pride for the fans. It’s the kind of clutch performance that defines a playoff team, and it’s hard not to see it as a sign of things to come.  

But let’s not overlook the offense. The D-backs have been a mess at times

optimist.name: Dolores 'Dolly' Sanderlin
optimist.column:
The D-backs didn’t just win yesterday—they danced through the fire. A walk-off loss in extras? That’s just the universe setting up a bigger comeback. Keep the pool open, we’re hosting October.  

You know the script: Phillies had it all figured out, leading 4-3 in the bottom of the ninth. D-backs’ bullpen, that ragtag band of misfits, steps in. Closer Daniel Bard, who’s been a wild card all season, gets the hook. Wait for it—Bard throws a slider, Phillies hit it, and it’s a double play. Then, a walk, a single, a sacrifice fly, and the D-backs tie it. The crowd roars, the sun dips behind the ballpark, and somehow, it feels like the desert itself is humming with possibility.  

This team? They’ve got that “we’re not done yet” vibe. Look at their record—8-6

pessimist.name: Jack Durmire
pessimist.column:
The D-backs won again. Five runs, a walk-off home run, and a bullpen that finally remembered how to throw strikes. You’d think that’d be enough to make a pessimist happy. But no. Not when the scoreboard reads 8-6 and the NL West is still a 2.5-game nightmare. Small-market excuses, sure—but you don’t need a spreadsheet to see the math doesn’t add up.  

Let’s talk about the bullpen. Seven innings of no-hit ball, then the bullpen shows up and it’s a Jeff Bagwell speech in the clubhouse. Same movie. Different Tuesday. This isn’t a new script. The D-backs have been playing this exact scenario for years, trading away prospects like they’re going out of style. Remember the Kendrick era? The front office traded away every decent arm in a 2015 blockbuster, and now we’re stuck with a bullpen that looks like it’s auditioning for a sitcom. The closer, the setup man, the long man—they all have the same fastball velocity. It’s like the team’s pitching staff is a group project where everyone’s supposed to do the same thing.  

And let’s not forget the offense. They’re not exactly hitting .250 with a .300 on-base percentage. They’re just good enough to win close games, bad enough to lose them when the stakes are high. The lineup has enough talent to make a playoff run, but
